subtitle: In the Interest of Science—and for Humanity!
summary: This opening section introduces the yellow fever story and its key players: Walter Reed, the courteous and logical head of the Yellow Fever Commission; James Carroll, a fearless ex-lumberjack; Jesse Lazear, who let a mosquito bite him and died; and brave volunteer soldiers. It also introduces Dr. Carlos Finlay, a Cuban doctor ridiculed as a fool for claiming mosquitoes caused yellow fever, at a time when nobody agreed on how the disease spread and the only accepted remedy was to flee infected towns.

segments.anchor_sentence: With yellow fever it was different—there were no brawls about it.

visual.scene_description: The key figures of the Yellow Fever Commission gathered in a sparse military office in Cuba: Walter Reed reviewing documents, James Carroll standing ready, and Jesse Lazear examining a mosquito on his hand, with volunteer soldiers visible through the doorway.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, three men in a bare wooden military office in Cuba, Walter Reed in US Army uniform with kind weathered face studying papers at a desk, James Carroll broad-shouldered standing with arms crossed, Jesse Lazear holding up his hand examining a small mosquito, bright tropical light through shuttered window, volunteer soldiers visible outside through open doorway

segments.anchor_sentence: It was a grand coöperative fight, that scotching of the yellow jack.

visual.scene_description: Dr. Carlos Finlay, an older Cuban doctor with mutton chop whiskers, holds up a glass jar containing a mosquito while surrounded by dismissive doctors who gesture in disagreement, each pointing in different directions with their own conflicting remedies.
